messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: hvac_systems_equipment_pages_9_13.pdf
Equipment class: chiller

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_0d14b19355182c8a", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "System Selection Criteria", "summary": "Design engineer must consider temperature, humidity, air motion, air/water velocity, water quality, outdoor air quality, indoor air purity, air changes, acoustics, climate, mold prevention, capacities, redundancy, spatial requirements, environmental health and safety, security, first cost, ROI, energy consumption, operator labor, maintenance costs, serviceability, reliability, flexibility, controllability, replaceability, life-cycle analysis, sustainability, seismic protection, filtration, and changing codes.", "structured_payload": {"title": "System Selection Criteria", "summary": "Design engineer must consider temperature, humidity, air motion, air/water velocity, water quality, outdoor air quality, indoor air purity, air changes, acoustics, climate, mold prevention, capacities, redundancy, spatial requirements, environmental health and safety, security, first cost, ROI, energy consumption, operator labor, maintenance costs, serviceability, reliability, flexibility, controllability, replaceability, life-cycle analysis, sustainability, seismic protection, filtration, and changing codes."}, "evidence": "1.1CHAPTER 1

N HVAC sys tem maintains desi red environmental conditionsA in a space.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_0de7a0a55a025531", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Constructability Constraints", "summary": "Constructability issues include existing conditions, rigging equipment, demolition impact, maintaining occupancy, phasing installation, temporary HVAC, equipment availability, construction schedule, and construction budget.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Constructability Constraints", "summary": "Constructability issues include existing conditions, rigging equipment, demolition impact, maintaining occupancy, phasing installation, temporary HVAC, equipment availability, construction schedule, and construction budget."}, "evidence": "This chapter addresses procedures for selecting
an appropriate system for a give n application while taking into
account pertinent issues associat ed with designing, building, com-
missioning, operating, and main taining the system. It also
addresses specific owner require ments and constraints associated
with selecting the optimum HVAC  system for the application.
Chapters 2  to 18 describe specif ic approaches and systems along
with their attributes, based on their heating and cooling medium,
the commonly used va riations, constructa bility, commissioning,
operation, and maintenance.
This chapter is intended as a guide for the design engineer,
builder, facility manage r, and student needing to know or reference
the analysis and selection process that ultimately leads to recom-mending the optimum system for the job.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_f408b40c3746ff05", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Decentralized System Characteristics", "summary": "Decentralized systems distribute cooling throughout the building, generally with direct-expansion cooling, electric or gas-fired heating, and occasionally oil-fired warm-air heating units.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Decentralized System Characteristics", "summary": "Decentralized systems distribute cooling throughout the building, generally with direct-expansion cooling, electric or gas-fired heating, and occasionally oil-fired warm-air heating units."}, "evidence": "1.1CHAPTER 1

N HVAC sys tem maintains desi red environmental conditionsA in a space.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_ca5b2edf8b960762", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "System Selection Matrix Scoring", "summary": "A numerical rating system such as 0 to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not applicable, can provide a quantitative result for system selection.", "structured_payload": {"title": "System Selection Matrix Scoring", "summary": "A numerical rating system such as 0 to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not applicable, can provide a quantitative result for system selection."}, "evidence": "In addition,
HVAC systems deemed inappropriate should be noted as having
been considered but not found appl icable to meet the owner’s pri-
mary HVAC goal.
The report should include an HVAC system selection matrix
that identifies the one or two suggested HVAC sy stem selections
(primary and secondary, when applic able), system c onstraints, and
other constraints and considerations. In completing this matrix
assessment, the design engineer should identify the owner’s input
to the analysis. This input can also  be applied as weighted multipli-
ers, because not all criteria ca rry the same weighted value.
Many grading methods are availabl e to complete an analytical
matrix analysis. Probably the simplest is to rate each item excel-
lent, very good, good, fair, or poor . A numerical rating system suchas 0 to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not
applicable, can provide a quantitative result.", "source_pages": [4], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_2ec788ef400eb7f3", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Decentralized System Capacity Requirements", "summary": "A decentralized system usually requires each piece of equipment to be sized for zone peak capacity, unless the systems are variable-volume, and does not benefit as much from equipment sizing diversity as centralized systems.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Decentralized System Capacity Requirements", "summary": "A decentralized system usually requires each piece of equipment to be sized for zone peak capacity, unless the systems are variable-volume, and does not benefit as much from equipment sizing diversity as centralized systems."}, "evidence": "1.1CHAPTER 1
